beats.text: A dataset is imbalanced when one class vastly outnumbers another, and the rare class is usually the one you care about.
Fraud runs near 1 transaction in 1000, so the minority prevalence pi can be as small as one tenth of one percent.
Majority-class accuracy equals one minus pi, so a constant predictor that detects nothing still scores almost perfectly.
On 100000 transactions with 100 frauds, always predicting legitimate reads 99.9 percent accurate while catching zero cases.
As you dial the minority share down, accuracy climbs toward 100 percent while recall on the rare class collapses.
Average-loss training lets the easy majority dominate the gradient, pushing the boundary into the minority region as an optimization bias.
The simplest cure changes the data itself, altering class proportions so the loss can no longer ignore the minority.
Random over-sampling duplicates minority examples until the classes match, keeping all data but inviting overfitting to exact copies.
Random under-sampling discards majority examples until balanced, which is fast and light but throws away useful majority data.
To reach a target minority share rho, grow the minority to rho over one minus rho times the majority count.

visual.note: EQ D5.2 — rho = 1/2 gives a 1:1 balance

beats.text: Over-sampling 50 minority up to 950 against 950 majority moves the minority share from 0.05 to exactly 0.5.
Resampling adds no information; the cardinal rule is to resample the training fold only and keep test data at real prevalence.
Random over-sampling copies points; SMOTE invents them by interpolating along the segment between a minority example and a neighbor.
A new point is x_i plus lambda times the gap to a near neighbor, with lambda drawn uniformly between zero and one.
With x_i at 2, a neighbor at 6, and lambda 0.25, the synthetic point lands a quarter of the way along at 3.
Every synthetic point lives inside the convex hull of the minority class, never extrapolating beyond the real examples.
Borderline-SMOTE synthesizes only near the decision boundary, while ADASYN pours more synthetic mass where the minority is most outnumbered.
SMOTE is a sharp tool, not a magic wand: it can amplify noise and blurs the boundary where classes overlap.

title: Algorithm-level fixes — class weights, focal loss, threshold moving

beats.text: Instead of rewriting the data, leave it alone and rewrite the objective with three loss- and decision-level levers.
Weighted cross-entropy scales each class by N over C times its count, so rarer classes get proportionally louder gradients.
For 1000 examples with 50 minority across two classes, balanced weighting gives the minority class a weight of 10.
Focal loss multiplies cross-entropy by one minus p_t to the gamma, deleting the gradient of easy, already-correct examples.

visual.note: EQ D5.5 — focal loss, paper used gamma = 2

beats.text: An easy example at p_t 0.9 with gamma 2 keeps only 0.01 of its loss, so the gradient flows to hard cases.
Threshold moving needs no retraining: when a miss costs 9 times a false alarm, the optimal cut drops to 0.1.

title: Evaluating under imbalance — PR curves, the right metric

beats.text: Every prediction falls into one of four confusion-matrix cells, and every honest metric is built from them.

visual.sub: PR curves and the right metric

beats.text: Precision asks how many alarms were real, recall asks how many real cases you caught, and neither uses true negatives.

visual.note: EQ D5.7 — precision and recall ignore TN

beats.text: Catching all 10 positives but raising 90 false alarms gives perfect recall yet a precision of just 0.1.
Screening 10000 people at 1 percent prevalence with 90 percent recall and 8 percent false-positive rate yields 792 false alarms.
That base-rate ambush drops precision to about 10 percent, and no amount of resampling can fix the prevalence speaking.
ROC-AUC stays flattering because false positives barely dent FPR, so on imbalanced problems prefer PR-AUC instead.
